questions.question_text: Which of the following elements is REQUIRED in a valid real estate purchase contract?
questions.correct_answer: Parties, property description, price, and signatures
distractors: A closing date, earnest money, and an inspection contingency
Notarization of signatures and recording in deed records
A pre-approval letter and proof of funds for the down payment
questions.explanation: A valid purchase contract requires the same elements as any valid contract (competent parties, mutual assent, consideration, lawful purpose, voluntary consent, in writing) plus a sufficient property description to identify what is being sold. A closing date, earnest money, and contingencies are common but not legally required — parties can agree to close 'as soon as possible' without a fixed date, and earnest money is customary but not mandated. Notarization and recording are required for deeds, not purchase contracts. Pre-approval letters and proof of funds are practical lender requirements, not contract validity requirements.

questions.question_text: A buyer submits an offer to purchase at 9:00 AM. At 11:00 AM, before the seller has responded, the buyer calls to withdraw the offer. The buyer:
questions.correct_answer: May revoke the offer
distractors: Cannot revoke, because an offer is binding once submitted
Cannot revoke until the seller formally rejects the offer
May revoke only with the listing broker’s consent
questions.explanation: An offer is freely revocable at any time before acceptance, even if the offer states it will remain open for a set period. This is the general rule under contract law: without consideration to keep the offer open (an option), the offeror can revoke at will. The offer must still be communicated to the offeree before revocation — if the seller has already mailed or communicated their acceptance before receiving the revocation, a contract may have formed under the 'mailbox rule.' An option (paid consideration to hold an offer open) is the exception that makes an offer irrevocable for the option period.

questions.question_text: A buyer defaults on a purchase contract and the seller retains the earnest money. The earnest money was held by the listing broker. Who is responsible for disbursing the earnest money to the seller?
questions.correct_answer: The broker, per contract terms or a mutual written release
distractors: The buyer's lender, since it was part of the financing package
The closing attorney or title company, regardless of who holds funds
The broker may disburse immediately upon the seller's verbal demand
questions.explanation: Earnest money is held in a broker's trust (escrow) account. The broker is a neutral stakeholder — they cannot disburse disputed funds based on one party's demand alone. Proper disbursement requires either: (1) a written release signed by both parties, (2) a court order, or (3) specific contract language that clearly triggers disbursement (e.g., a default clause). Improper disbursement of trust funds — even to the rightful party without proper authorization — can constitute a license law violation. Brokers who disburse without authorization risk license discipline regardless of who 'deserved' the money.

questions.question_text: Earnest money is BEST described as:
questions.correct_answer: A good-faith deposit showing the buyer's serious intent
distractors: The legal consideration required to make a contract binding
A partial down payment always applied to closing costs
A penalty assessed if the buyer exercises a contingency
questions.explanation: Earnest money is a good-faith deposit made by the buyer to demonstrate serious intent. It is not legally required for contract validity — consideration for a purchase contract is the mutual exchange of promises (buyer's promise to pay; seller's promise to convey). Earnest money is typically applied toward the purchase price or closing costs at settlement, but the specific treatment depends on the contract. Exercising a valid contingency is not a default — if a contingency allows the buyer to cancel, they typically receive their earnest money back. Earnest money is forfeited only when the buyer defaults without a valid contractual basis for cancellation.

questions.question_text: When a buyer signs a purchase contract, they immediately acquire:
questions.correct_answer: Equitable title
distractors: Legal title
A lien against the property
No property interest
questions.explanation: Under the doctrine of equitable conversion, when a binding purchase contract is signed, equitable title passes to the buyer and legal title (the right to receive the deed) remains with the seller until closing. The buyer's equitable title is a real property interest — it can be insured, liened, and in some states the buyer bears the risk of loss if the property is damaged before closing. Legal title only passes when the deed is delivered. This distinction matters when a party dies between contract and closing: equitable title passes through the buyer's estate, while legal title (deed authority) remains in the seller's estate.

questions.question_text: A buyer pays $3,000 for a 60-day option to purchase a property at $275,000. During the option period, the buyer decides NOT to exercise the option. The $3,000:
questions.correct_answer: Is forfeited to the seller
distractors: Must be refunded since no purchase occurred
Is credited against the seller's marketing expenses
Must be held in escrow until the 60-day period expires
questions.explanation: Option consideration is the payment that makes the option contract binding and keeps the offer open for the option period. It is generally non-refundable — the seller earned it by taking the property off the market (or forgoing other opportunities) during the option period. The buyer paid for the right to decide, not for the obligation to buy. If the buyer exercises the option, the consideration may or may not be credited toward the purchase price, depending on the contract terms. If the option is not exercised, the seller keeps the option money as compensation for the exclusivity period.

questions.question_text: A purchase contract contains a financing contingency stating the contract is contingent on the buyer obtaining a 30-year fixed mortgage at 7% or less within 21 days. The buyer applies but is only approved at 7.5%. The buyer:
questions.correct_answer: May cancel the contract and receive their earnest money back
distractors: Must proceed with the purchase at 7.5%
May cancel but forfeits the earnest money for failing to secure financing
Must request an extension before invoking the contingency
questions.explanation: A financing contingency protects the buyer by allowing them to cancel without penalty if they cannot obtain financing on the specified terms. Here, the contract requires a rate at or below 7%, and the buyer was only approved at 7.5% — the contingency condition was not met. The buyer may properly invoke the contingency, cancel the contract, and recover the earnest money. The buyer is not required to accept different terms than specified. However, buyers who use financing contingencies in bad faith (e.g., not actually applying for a loan) may be treated as defaulting and lose their earnest money.

questions.question_text: A buyer's purchase contract includes an inspection contingency. The inspection reveals a cracked foundation. The buyer may do any of the following EXCEPT:
questions.correct_answer: Demand the seller reduce the price by the exact repair cost
distractors: Cancel under the contingency and recover the earnest money
Accept the property as-is and waive the inspection contingency
Request the seller negotiate repairs or a price reduction
questions.explanation: An inspection contingency gives the buyer the right to cancel if the inspection reveals unsatisfactory conditions — it does not give the buyer the legal right to demand specific repairs or a particular price reduction. The buyer may: (1) cancel and recover earnest money, (2) accept as-is, or (3) negotiate a resolution (repairs, price reduction, or credits). The key word is 'negotiate' — both parties must agree to any modification. If the seller refuses to repair or reduce the price, the buyer's only contractual right is to cancel, not to compel specific remediation.

questions.question_text: The property description in a purchase contract should be:
questions.correct_answer: Sufficient to identify the specific property
distractors: Always a metes and bounds description by a licensed surveyor
The same description on the seller's most recent tax bill
Not necessary if both parties know which property is being sold
questions.explanation: A purchase contract must identify the property with enough specificity that a court could determine which property the parties intended. A full legal description (lot/block, metes and bounds, or government survey) is ideal, but a street address is usually sufficient for a purchase contract. The deed, however, should contain the full legal description. The key is that the description must be unambiguous — if two properties could match the description, the contract may be unenforceable for vagueness.

questions.question_text: A purchase contract that does not specify a closing date is:
questions.correct_answer: Still valid
distractors: Void
Voidable
Unenforceable
questions.explanation: While a closing date is customary and highly recommended, it is not a legally required element for contract validity. If no date is specified, the law implies a 'reasonable time' for performance. The essential elements are: competent parties, mutual assent, consideration, lawful purpose, and a sufficient property description. The Statute of Frauds requires the contract to be in writing but does not mandate a specific closing date. In practice, nearly all purchase contracts include a closing date to avoid ambiguity.

questions.question_text: Which of the following would make a purchase contract unenforceable?
questions.correct_answer: It is not signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought
distractors: It does not include an earnest money deposit provision
It was prepared by a real estate agent instead of an attorney
It uses a street address instead of a full legal description
questions.explanation: Under the Statute of Frauds, a real estate contract must be signed by the party to be charged (the party against whom enforcement is sought). Without their signature, the contract cannot be enforced against them. Earnest money is customary but not required for validity. In most states, real estate agents may fill in blanks on approved contract forms (not practice law). A street address is generally sufficient to identify the property for contract purposes, though the deed should contain the full legal description.

questions.question_text: Personal property items included in a real estate sale should be:
questions.correct_answer: Specifically listed in the purchase contract to avoid disputes
distractors: Assumed to transfer with the real property under state law
Excluded from the contract and handled in a separate bill of sale
Covered by the general warranty deed conveying all premises items
questions.explanation: Personal property (appliances, window treatments, furniture) does not automatically transfer with real property unless it has become a fixture. To avoid disputes, any personal property the buyer expects to receive should be explicitly listed in the purchase contract. Items like refrigerators, washers/dryers, and above-ground pools are common sources of conflict. A warranty deed conveys real property and fixtures, not personal property. While a bill of sale may also be used, listing items in the purchase contract ensures both parties agree before closing.

questions.question_text: Under the 'mailbox rule,' an acceptance is effective when:
questions.correct_answer: It is dispatched by the offeree, not when received by the offeror
distractors: It is received and read by the offeror at their business
It is postmarked on or before the offer's expiration date
Both parties have confirmed receipt by signed acknowledgment
questions.explanation: The mailbox rule (also called the deposited acceptance rule) states that an acceptance is effective upon dispatch — when the offeree sends it (drops it in the mail, hits send on an email, etc.). This is an exception to the general rule that communications are effective upon receipt. The rationale is that the offeree should be able to rely on having formed a contract the moment they send their acceptance. Note: revocations, rejections, and counteroffers are effective upon receipt, not upon dispatch. The mailbox rule applies only to acceptances.

questions.question_text: A seller receives two identical offers on the same property at the same time. The seller:
questions.correct_answer: May accept either offer
distractors: Must accept the offer with the higher earnest money deposit
Must present both offers to a neutral third party for determination
Must reject both and relist to allow additional competing offers
questions.explanation: A seller is free to accept, reject, or counter any offer. There is no legal requirement to accept the highest offer, the first offer received, or any offer at all. The seller may choose based on any criteria: price, terms, contingencies, closing timeline, financing strength, or personal preference. The listing agent has a duty to present all offers to the seller (in most states), but the decision rests entirely with the seller. Multiple-offer situations are common in competitive markets.

questions.question_text: A buyer submits an offer with an expiration time. The seller does not respond by the deadline. The offer:
questions.correct_answer: Expires automatically
distractors: Remains open indefinitely until the seller formally rejects it
Is automatically accepted
Converts to a counteroffer from the seller at the original terms
questions.explanation: An offer expires if not accepted by the stated deadline. Silence or inaction by the offeree is never acceptance — mutual assent requires a definitive, communicated acceptance. If the seller wants to accept after the deadline, their late acceptance is treated as a new offer (counteroffer) from the seller to the buyer, which the buyer can then accept or reject. An offer without a stated deadline remains open for a 'reasonable time' determined by the circumstances.

questions.question_text: A buyer submits an offer. The seller responds, 'I accept your offer, but I need an extra week to move out.' This response is:
questions.correct_answer: A counteroffer
distractors: A valid acceptance
A conditional acceptance that becomes binding after one business day
An inquiry
questions.explanation: Under the mirror image rule, acceptance must match the offer exactly. Adding a condition — even a seemingly minor one like extra time to move out — creates a counteroffer, not an acceptance. The original offer is terminated. The buyer must now decide whether to accept the seller's modified terms. If the seller had simply said 'I accept,' a contract would have formed. The added condition, however small, transforms the response into a counteroffer.

questions.question_text: An earnest money dispute arises between the buyer and seller. Neither party will sign a release. The broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Hold funds in trust until receiving a court order or mutual release
distractors: Disburse to the seller since they are the listing client
Disburse to the buyer since the buyer deposited the money
Deduct the brokerage commission and split the remainder
questions.explanation: When earnest money is disputed and neither party will sign a release, the broker must hold the funds. The broker cannot unilaterally decide who gets the money — doing so risks license discipline. Options include: holding until the parties reach agreement, interpleading (filing a court action asking the judge to decide), or following state-specific dispute resolution procedures. The broker may never use disputed earnest money to pay their commission. Some states have specific timelines for how long a broker can hold disputed funds before interpleading.

questions.question_text: Within how many days must a broker typically deposit earnest money into their trust account after receiving it?
questions.correct_answer: Within a few business days
distractors: Within 30 calendar days of receiving the funds
No deposit deadline exists
Immediately upon receipt
questions.explanation: State laws vary, but most require brokers to deposit earnest money promptly — typically within 1 to 5 business days of receipt (some states specify 'next business day'). Holding earnest money without depositing it is a trust account violation in most states. The exact deadline varies by jurisdiction, which is why the exam tests the concept of prompt deposit rather than a specific number of days. Commingling (mixing earnest money with personal or business funds) is a serious license violation.

questions.question_text: Commingling of earnest money occurs when a broker:
questions.correct_answer: Mixes client trust funds with personal or business operating funds
distractors: Deposits earnest money from two transactions into one trust account
Accepts earnest money as a personal check instead of certified funds
Holds earnest money for more than 30 days without disbursing
questions.explanation: Commingling is mixing client funds (earnest money, security deposits, rent collections) with the broker's own personal or business funds. This is a serious license violation in every state. A broker must maintain a separate trust (escrow) account for client funds. Depositing multiple clients' earnest money into the same trust account is acceptable — that is the purpose of the trust account. Accepting personal checks and holding funds during a dispute are not commingling. The broker may keep a small amount of personal funds in the trust account to cover bank fees (varies by state).

questions.question_text: The doctrine of equitable conversion means that between contract and closing:
questions.correct_answer: The buyer holds equitable title and the seller retains legal title
distractors: The buyer has no property interest until the deed is delivered
Both parties hold joint title until the transaction closes
The seller loses all rights the moment the contract is signed
questions.explanation: Equitable conversion is a legal doctrine that recognizes the buyer's interest in the property from the moment a binding purchase contract is signed. The buyer's equitable title gives them the right to acquire legal title upon performance. The seller retains legal title (and the right to possess the property) until closing when the deed is delivered. This doctrine also affects risk of loss in some states — if the property is damaged before closing, the buyer may bear the risk because they hold equitable title.

questions.question_text: A buyer signs a purchase contract on March 1. The seller dies on March 15. Closing was scheduled for April 1. What happens to the contract?
questions.correct_answer: The contract survives
distractors: The contract is automatically void
The contract becomes voidable
The contract is suspended until a probate court appoints a representative
questions.explanation: Under equitable conversion, the buyer acquired equitable title when the contract was signed. The seller's death does not terminate the contract — the obligation to convey title passes to the seller's estate or heirs. The personal representative (executor/administrator) must deliver the deed at closing. Similarly, if the buyer dies, the buyer's estate must complete the purchase. This is because the buyer's equitable interest is a property right that passes through the estate, not a personal obligation that dies with the person.

questions.question_text: A buyer who has signed a purchase contract but has not yet closed holds:
questions.correct_answer: An insurable interest in the property
distractors: No interest in the property
A lien interest
Fee simple ownership
questions.explanation: A buyer's equitable title creates an insurable interest. The buyer can (and should) obtain hazard insurance effective from the contract date, because in some jurisdictions the risk of loss shifts to the buyer at contract. Even in jurisdictions where the seller bears the risk until closing, the buyer has a financial interest worth protecting. The buyer does not yet have legal title (fee simple) — that comes at closing. The buyer's interest is not a lien — it is an equitable ownership interest.

questions.question_text: Equitable title differs from legal title in that equitable title:
questions.correct_answer: Gives the right to acquire ownership but does not convey the deed
distractors: Can only be held by corporations and trusts, not individuals
Is the same as having a recorded deed in the buyer's name
Provides no legal protection and cannot be enforced
questions.explanation: Equitable title is the beneficial interest in property — the right to obtain legal title upon fulfilling contract conditions (usually paying the purchase price). Legal title is actual ownership evidenced by a recorded deed. The buyer's equitable interest is enforceable — they can sue for specific performance if the seller refuses to close. It can also be sold, assigned, or encumbered. Equitable title is available to all buyers (individuals, corporations, trusts) and provides meaningful legal protection.

questions.question_text: An option contract differs from a right of first refusal in that an option:
questions.correct_answer: Gives the holder the right to buy at a set price
distractors: Requires the seller to actively market the property first
Is only available for commercial properties, not residential
Gives the holder the right to match any third-party offer
questions.explanation: An option gives the holder the unilateral right to purchase at a predetermined price during the option period — no triggering event is needed. A right of first refusal (ROFR) gives the holder the right to match a third-party offer before the seller can accept it — it only activates when the seller receives a bona fide offer. Options are more valuable because the holder controls the timing. ROFRs are reactive. Both can be used for residential or commercial properties. The holder of an option pays option consideration; ROFR holders may or may not pay consideration.

questions.question_text: During the option period, the property owner who granted the option:
questions.correct_answer: Cannot sell the property to anyone else
distractors: May sell to a higher bidder and refund the option consideration
Can revoke the option by returning double the option consideration
Must continue marketing and present any competing offers received
questions.explanation: An option is irrevocable during the option period because the holder paid consideration to keep the offer open. The seller is bound — they cannot sell to another buyer, revoke the option, or withdraw the property from the market during the option term. This is the key difference from a regular offer, which can be revoked at any time before acceptance. If the seller violates the option by selling to someone else, the option holder can sue for specific performance or damages.

questions.question_text: A tenant has a right of first refusal in their lease. The landlord receives a bona fide offer from a third party. The tenant:
questions.correct_answer: Must be given the opportunity to match the offer first
distractors: Has the right to purchase at 10% below the third-party offer
Automatically acquires equitable title when the offer is received
Must waive their right in writing before the landlord can sell
questions.explanation: A right of first refusal gives the holder the right to match (not beat) a third-party offer. When the landlord receives a bona fide offer, they must notify the tenant and give them a reasonable time to decide whether to match the offer's price and terms. If the tenant matches, they purchase the property. If the tenant declines or fails to respond, the landlord can sell to the third party. The tenant does not get a discount — they must match the exact terms. The right does not give automatic ownership.

questions.question_text: A buyer holds a valid option to purchase a property. Which statement correctly describes the parties' obligations during the option period?
questions.correct_answer: The optionor must sell if the option is exercised, but the optionee has no obligation to buy
distractors: Both parties are bound to complete the sale as soon as the option is signed
The optionee must purchase the property before the option period expires
Neither party is bound until the option is exercised
questions.explanation: An option creates a one-sided set of obligations: the optionor (owner) is bound to sell at the agreed price if the option is exercised and cannot revoke during the option period, while the optionee (buyer) holds a right but no obligation — they may exercise the option or simply let it expire, forfeiting only the option consideration. If the optionee exercises the option, a bilateral purchase contract arises on the stated terms and both parties become obligated to close. This asymmetry — only one party bound from the outset — is why an unexercised option is classified as a unilateral contract.

questions.question_text: An appraisal contingency protects the buyer by allowing cancellation if:
questions.correct_answer: The property appraises below the purchase price
distractors: The appraiser finds physical defects during the visit
The appraisal takes longer than 30 days to complete
The seller refuses to allow the appraiser access
questions.explanation: An appraisal contingency allows the buyer to cancel if the appraised value comes in below the contract price. This matters because lenders base loan amounts on the lower of the purchase price or appraised value — a low appraisal means the buyer may need to bring more cash or renegotiate the price. If the appraisal meets or exceeds the purchase price, the contingency is satisfied. The appraiser evaluates market value, not physical defects (that's the home inspection). There is no standard 30-day appraisal deadline in the contingency.

questions.question_text: A purchase contract is contingent on the sale of the buyer's current home. This contingency:
questions.correct_answer: Allows the buyer to cancel if their home does not sell in time
distractors: Requires the seller to help market the buyer's current home
Guarantees the buyer will close regardless of their home's sale
Gives the seller the right to cancel if a better offer arrives
questions.explanation: A sale contingency (also called a 'hubbard' or 'home sale' contingency) protects buyers who need proceeds from selling their current home to purchase a new one. If the buyer's home does not sell within the agreed timeframe, the buyer can cancel and recover their earnest money. Many sellers accept these contingencies with a 'kick-out' clause — the seller can continue to market the property and, if another offer comes in, give the contingent buyer a deadline (e.g., 72 hours) to remove their contingency or lose the contract.

questions.question_text: A buyer waives all contingencies in their offer. This means the buyer:
questions.correct_answer: Has no contractual right to cancel
distractors: Can still cancel within 72 hours under federal cooling-off rules
Will automatically receive a lower price in exchange for added risk
Is prohibited from obtaining a home inspection before closing
questions.explanation: Waiving contingencies makes the offer stronger for the seller but riskier for the buyer. Without contingencies, the buyer cannot cancel for inspection issues, low appraisal, or financing failure without risking their earnest money. There is no federal 72-hour cooling-off period for real estate purchases (that applies to door-to-door sales under the FTC rule and certain refinance transactions under TILA). The buyer can still get an inspection — they just cannot cancel based on the results. Waiving contingencies does not automatically reduce the price.
